page_content: karl marx viewed crime as a result of economic inequality inherent in capitalist societies. - the rich control resources and lawmaking. - poverty and exploitation push the poor toward crime. - laws protect capitalist interests and criminalize working-class behavior. example: property crimes and thefts arise from deprivation; white-collar crimes from greed. implications: true crime reduction requires economic justice and redistribution of wealth. runciman and others (1960s) suggested that crime stems from perceived inequality - people compare themselves with others and feel deprived. key idea: even in affluent societies, unequal distribution of resources causes resentment, leading to theft, fraud, or violence. example: youth in slums comparing themselves with the middle class may turn to gangs or drug trade. this theory applies economic logic to crime. people commit crimes when expected benefits exceed expected costs. formula: if (expected utility of crime > utility of legal action), crime is chosen. example: a person may engage in tax evasion if chances of being caught are low and gains are high. policy implication: increase cost (punishment certainty, severity) and reduce benefit (target hardening). cohen & felson (1979) proposed that crime occurs when: - motivated offender - suitable target - lack of capable guardian economic conditions shape availability of targets and surveillance capacity. example: urbanization and consumerism create more opportunities for theft. (e) economic strain and unemployment: economic instability, joblessness, and poverty increase criminal behavior. during economic recessions, rates of burglary, robbery, and fraudrise. this link shows that economic stress correlates with social instability . - provide quantifiable, data-based explanations. - link macroeconomic trends (inflation, poverty) to crime. - useful for policy formulation in urban planning, welfare, and labor. - show that crime prevention requires economic opportunity and social equality. - reduces human behavior to monetary calculation. - ignores emotional, moral, or psychological motives. - not all poor individuals commit crimes; not all rich avoid them. - overlooks crimes without economic motive (e.g., passion crimes). modern criminologists integrate economic factors with psychological and sociological perspectives, forming multifactorial models: - economic stress psychological frustration deviance (strain theory). - unemployment low self-esteem criminal opportunity seeking. this holistic view bridges material and mental dimensions of crime. focus, psychological theories = mental processes, personality, emotions. focus, economic theories = material conditions, inequality, opportunity. causation, psychological theories = internal (mind, personality). causation, economic theories = external (poverty, deprivation). key thinkers, psychological theories = freud, eysenck, bandura, kohlberg. key thinkers, economic theories = marx, becker, cohen & felson. nature of crime, psychological theories = pathological or learned behavior. nature of crime, economic theories = rational or necessity-driven act. methods, psychological theories = clinical, experimental, behavioral. methods, economic theories = statistical, econometric, rational analysis

page_content: policy implications, psychological theories = therapy, counseling, rehabilitation. policy implications, economic theories = economic reform, job creation, deterrence. weaknesses, psychological theories = ignores social context. weaknesses, economic theories = ignores psychological motives these theories emerged in different historical, economic, and sociopolitical contexts, but they collectively enrich criminological analysis by offering multidimensional views - biological, social, psychological, and political. radical criminology emerged during the late 1960s and early 1970s, influenced by marxist ideology, social unrest, and the civil rights movements. it challenged traditional criminological approaches that focused solely on the individual offender and instead analyzed crime through the lens of power, inequality, and capitalism. it is sometimes referred to as marxist criminology, emphasizing that the roots of crime lie in the structure of capitalist societies. radical criminology draws from karl marx's ideas about class conflict and economic exploitation. marx argued that laws serve the interests of the ruling class - the bourgeoisie - and function as tools to control the proletariat or working class. thus, crime is not merely an individual moral failure; it is a social product of capitalist inequality and oppression. - william bonger - early marxist criminologist who linked crime to capitalist greed and economic inequality. - richard quinney - proposed that laws are created to protect capitalist interests and maintain class structure. - ian taylor, paul walton, and jock young - authors of 'the new criminology' (1973), who developed critical criminology, integrating marxism with interactionism and sociology. - crime as a social construction - crime is defined by those in power to maintain dominance. - law as an instrument of class rule - legal systems criminalize acts threatening to capitalist order. - inequality and alienation - economic deprivation and alienation drive individuals toward crime. - corporate and state crime - crimes of the powerful (white-collar, corporate, or governmental crimes) often go unpunished. - social revolution - true crime reduction requires restructuring society to eliminate exploitation. - overemphasis on capitalism; ignores crime in socialist states. - downplays individual responsibility and morality. - often lacks empirical evidence. - idealistic - assumes crime would disappear with socialism. despite these criticisms, radical criminology's legacy lies in emphasizing social justice, economic reform, and structural causes of crime. modern relevance: modern critical criminology - including feminist criminology, green criminology, and postmodern criminology - draws heavily from radical criminology, extending its critique of power, privilege, and inequality. the labelling theory (or social reaction theory) emerged in the 1960s as a challenge to positivist criminology. it focuses on how society's reaction to deviance contributes to the continuation of criminal behavior. rather than asking, ' why do people commit crime?', it asks, 'how does society create and sustain deviance?' theoretical background: labelling theory developed from the symbolic interactionist school of sociology (mead, cooley, blumer), emphasizing that social reality is constructed through interactions. crime and deviance, therefore, are not inherent qualities of an act but are defined through social reaction and stigmatization. - frank tannenbaum (1938) - introduced the concept of 'tagging,' where minor deviance labeled by society leads to a criminal identity. - edwin lemert (1951) - distinguished between primary and secondary deviance: - primary deviance: initial, often minor rule-breaking. - secondary deviance: result of societal reaction and internalized deviant label. - howard becker (1963) - in ' outsiders', argued that deviance is not the act itself but the result of the label applied by society. - erving goffman (1968) - studied stigma and how labels affect self-concept.

page_content: - social reaction defines crime - acts are deviant only when society labels them so. - label identity deviance - labeling alters self-concept and social identity. - secondary deviance - stigmatized individuals may adopt deviant roles permanently. - selective enforcement - marginalized groups are more likely to be labeled as criminals. - self-fulfilling prophecy - once labeled, individuals are treated as criminals, reinforcing deviance. - advocates decriminalization of victimless crimes. - emphasizes rehabilitation and diversion programs over incarceration. - promotes restorative justice to reduce stigma. - fails to explain why primary deviance occurs. - overemphasizes the role of societal reaction. - neglects biological or psychological factors. - doesn't address serious crimes like murder or terrorism. nonetheless, labelling theory profoundly influenced modern juvenile justice, rehabilitation, and community-based corrections. biological theories propose that criminal behavior is rooted in an individual's biological and genetic makeup rather than just social or psychological factors. originating in the 19th century, these theories evolved from deterministic views (like lombroso's) to modern biosocial perspectives that integrate genetics, neurobiology, and environment. known as the 'father of modern criminology,' lombroso proposed the atavistic criminal theory - that criminals are evolutionary throwbacks with primitive physical traits. - criminals identified through physical stigmata (e.g., large jaws, asymmetrical faces). - classified offenders as: - born criminals - insane criminals - criminals by passion - occasional criminals although discredited today, lombroso's work introduced the scientific study of the criminal. - ernst kretschmer (1921) - linked body types to crime tendencies: - asthenic - petty criminals - athletic - violent offenders - william sheldon (1949) -somatotypes theory: - endomorph (soft, sociable) - mesomorph (muscular, aggressive - most likely to commit crime) - ectomorph (introverted, restrained) modern research studies the role of heredity in crime: - family studies - show higher criminality among relatives of offenders. - twin studies - higher concordance in identical twins for antisocial behavior. - adoption studies - children of criminal biological parents show higher risk even when raised by law-abiding families. genes may influence aggression, impulsivity, and risk-taking - but environmental factors moderate their impact. these explore how brain structure, chemistry, and hormones affect criminality. - prefrontal cortex dysfunction: linked to poor impulse control. - low serotonin levels: associated with aggression. - high testosterone: related to violent crimes. - neurological damage: brain injuries may lead to antisocial behavior.

page_content: contemporary biological theories integrate social, environmental, and biological factors: - interaction between genes and environment (gxe). - influence of prenatal nutrition, substance abuse, and stress. - emphasis on early childhood development and prevention. - deterministic and reductionist. - risk of stigmatization or racial bias. - overlooks social, economic, and cultural dimensions. - modern research stresses interaction - not isolation - of biology with environment. economic & class inequality, radical criminology = social reaction & stigma. economic & class inequality, labelling theory = genetic & physiological factors. economic & class inequality, biological theory = focus. marx, quinney, taylor, walton, young, radical criminology = becker, lemert, tannenbaum. marx, quinney, taylor, walton, young, labelling theory = lombroso, sheldon, eysenck. marx, quinney, taylor, walton, young, biological theory = key thinkers. product of capitalism, radical criminology = product of labeling process. product of capitalism, labelling theory = product of biological defects. product of capitalism, biological theory = view of crime. social reform & equality, radical criminology = de-stigmatization & rehabilitation. social reform & equality, labelling theory = medical or therapeutic treatment. social reform & equality, biological theory = solution. idealistic, class bias, radical criminology = ignores primary deviance. idealistic, class bias, labelling theory = overly deterministic. idealistic, class bias, biological theory = criticism * * * crime is a universal phenomenon that exists in every society. it refers to any human conduct that violates the law and is punishable by the state. the concept of crime varies according to the cultural, social, and legal framework of each society, yet it is always considered a harmful act that disturbs peace, order, and security. in the modern world, crimes have become increasingly complex due to technological advancements, economic disparities, and changing social values. criminology, as a scientific study of crime and criminal behavior, helps us understand the causes, nature, and types of crimes in order to prevent and control them effectively. crimes can be classified in several ways - based on their nature, motives, victims , means used, and social impact. in this lecture, we focus on four broad categories of crime: 1. traditional crimes 2. social crimes 3. family - centered crimes 4. modern crimes each of these categories represents a different dimension of human behaviour and social response to law and morality. meaning: traditional crimes are the oldest and most common forms of criminal behavior known to human civilization. they involve direct harm to individuals or property and have been recognized as offenses for centuries. these crimes are generally motivated by greed, anger, revenge, or passion, and they threaten the safety of individuals and communities. traditional crimes are also known as conventional crimes because they form the basic foundation of criminal law in every society. - murder - theft - robbery

page_content: - assault - kidnapping - burglary - rape - arson characteristics: personal and direct in nature: traditional crimes usually involve direct confrontation between offender and victim. physical harm or property loss: they result in bodily injury, death, or damage to property. easily identifiable: these crimes are recognized universally as wrong and punishable. punishable under penal codes: most penal codes worldwide, such as the indian penal code (ipc), focus on these crimes. driven by basic motives: such as greed, jealousy, revenge, or hatred. - (a) crimes against persons: these crimes are directed against the physical body or personal freedom of individuals. - murder (section 302 ipc): the unlawful killing of a person with intent to cause death. - assault (section 351 ipc): the act of inflicting physical harm or threatening to do so. - rape (section 375 ipc): sexual assault against the will of a woman. - kidnapping and abduction (sections 359-374 ipc): taking away a person by force or deceit. - (b) crimes against property: these involve the unlawful taking, destruction, or damage of another person's property. - theft (section 378 ipc): dishonestly taking someone's movable property. - robbery (section 390 ipc): theft accompanied by violence or threat. - dacoity (section 391 ipc): robbery committed by a group of five or more persons. - arson (section 435 ipc): setting fire to property with malicious intent. - (c) crimes against morality: these are acts considered immoral or indecent in society. - prostitution-related offenses - public obscenity - drunken disorderliness poverty and unemployment illiteracy and ignorance alcohol and drug abuse psychological disorders broken family structures peer pressure and revenge motives - strengthening law enforcement agencies - promoting education and employment - community policing - moral education and value-based upbringing - quick and fair judicial process meaning: social crimes are those offenses that harm society as a whole, rather than an individual. they violate the collective moral, ethical, and legal values of the community. these crimes are often linked to social evils, inequality, discrimination, and cultural backwardness. social crimes are not always violent, but they have deep social implications because they affect the moral fabric and progress of society. - dowry system - child marriage - caste discrimination - domestic violence - human trafficking - gender inequality - beggary and bonded labour socially destructive : they weaken community harmony and human dignity. linked to customs and traditions: many social crimes arise from outdated or unjust social practices. often normalized: some societies fail to recognize these acts as crimes due to cultural acceptance.

page_content: 1. medical assistance: victims are taken to medical facilities for treatment and para legal volunteers coordinate with medical professionals to ensure timely care and preparation of mlr. 2. police station assistance: victims receive help with fir registration, obtaining police records and navigating the police system. 3. legal aid and assistance : victims will be provided with necessary guidance on their legal rights, ensuring that their voices are heard. this assistance includes help with filing claims and understanding court procedures. 4. tie-up with other agencies: victims receive assistance in dealing with insurance claims, banks and other external agencies. 5. safety plan: the victim assistance force will inform victims about the witness protection scheme if their safety is at risk. 6. victim compensation scheme: ensures that eligible victims are guided through the process of applying for financial compensation under the victim compensation schemes (vcs), by the government authorities ensuring timely disbursement. 7. court accompaniment: victims are assisted with immediate legal representation and updating them about the progress of the case including dates fixed in the case, orders passed by the court on each date. 8. rehabilitation: necessary guidance will be provided to help the victims to rehabilitate through the appropriate government department/agencies so as to ensure that victims rebuild their lives with resilience and selfconfidence. in a victim-oriented approach, the police prioritize the needs and rights of crime victims, viewing them as individuals deserving of support and protection. this involves treating victims with respect, providing them with information about the legal process, and ensuring they are involved in the investigation and prosecution. the police also play a crucial role in connecting victims with resources and support services to help them heal and recover from the trauma of crime. - prioritizing victim needs: police focus on the needs and well-being of victims, rather than solely on the case or the offender. - rights-based approach: recognizing that victims have specific rights, such as the right to be informed, the right to be involved, and the right to be treated with respect. working with other agencies and professionals, such as victim advocates, counselors, and social workers, to provide comprehensive support to victims. - empowerment and support: providing victims with the information and resources they need to navigate the criminal justice system and to heal from the trauma of crime. - focus on healing: a victim-oriented approach recognizes that crime can cause significant psychological and emotional harm to victims, and aims to support their healing process. - victim advocacy: providing victims with assistance in navigating the criminal justice system, including helping them understand their rights, prepare for court, and access support services. - victim support programs: offering resources such as crisis intervention, counseling, and legal assistance to victims of crime. - trauma-informed policing: training police officers to understand the effects of trauma on victims and to respond in a sensitive and supportive manner. - family violence units: specially trained units within police departments that respond to family violence incidents and provide support to victims. - community-based policing: establishing strong relationships with community members and organizations to address crime prevention and victim support needs. under the probation of offenders act, 1958, a court can order an offender released on probation to pay compensation for losses or injuries caused by the crime. this order can also include the costs of the trial. the court determines the reasonable amount of compensation and costs. - power of the court: the court, when releasing an offender on probation (under section 3 or 4 of the act), has the power to order the offender to pay compensation and costs. - compensation and costs: the court can order the offender to pay compensation for losses or injuries caused by the offense and also the costs of the proceedings. - reasonable amount: the court will determine the reasonable amount of compensation and costs to be paid by the offender. - recovery: the amount ordered to be paid can be recovered as a fine, according to the code of criminal procedure.

page_content: - civil suits: a civil court hearing a suit arising from the same matter will consider any compensation paid under the act when awarding damages. under the motor vehicles act, compensation to victims of motor vehicle accidents, including hit-and-run cases, is primarily governed by section 163a. this section mandates a minimum compensation of 50,000 for death and 25,000 for permanent disability. furthermore, a new scheme for hit-andrun accidents, effective from april-1, 2022, offers enhanced compensation of rs.2,00,000 for death and rs.50,000 for grievous hurt. - section 163a: provides for a minimum compensation of 50,000 for death and 25,000 for permanent disability in the case of an accident. - hit-and-run scheme (2022): offers a higher compensation of 2,00,000 for death and 50,000 for grievous hurt in hit-and-run cases where the vehicle cannot be identified. the act also covers compensation for other types of injuries sustained in motor vehicle accidents, with the amount determined by the motor accidents claims tribunal (mact) based on the specific circumstances of the case. - hit-and-run cases: victims of hit-and-run accidents can claim compensation under the new scheme (2022), where the vehicle is unidentified. - other accidents: claims can be filed with the mact for compensation in other motor vehicle accidents. sc/st victims of atrocities in india can receive compensation under the scheduled castes and the scheduled tribes (prevention of atrocities) act, 1989. the compensation amounts vary depending on the nature of the crime and the victim's status, ranging from rs.85,000 to rs.8.5 lakh. the ministry of social justice and empowerment also provides relief and rehabilitation for victims of atrocities. - minimum relief: at least rs.85,000 for sc/st victims of certain atrocities like denial of voting rights, or any form of violence during elections. - victimization by public servant: rs.2 lakh for victimization of sc/st persons by a public servant. - social/economic boycott: rs.1 lakh for social or economic boycott of dalit individuals. - denial of entry: rs.1 lakh plus restoration of rights for denying entry to educational institutions, hospitals, or public places. - death or injury: compensation varies based on the nature of the crime, with amounts ranging from rs.2 lakh to rs.8 lakh. - property damage: compensation is also provided for property damage resulting from atrocities. compensation is often provided in installments: - 50% on fir/medical examination. - 25% when the charge sheet is sent to the court. - 25% when the accused are convicted by the lower court. - reimbursement of medical expenses: district magistrates can reimburse medical expenses, including medicines, special medical consultation, blood transfusion, and essential supplies. - relief for dependents: compensation is also provided for the dependents of victims who have died due to atrocities. - restoration of rights: in cases of denial of entry to public places or institutions, the law also provides for the immediate restoration of the victim's rights. the compensation part of the rehabilitation of victims of violence including rape is governed by provision of section 357a of the code of criminal procedure which states that every state government in co-ordination with the central government shall prepare a scheme for providing funds for the purpose of compensation to the victim of crime. so far 24 states and 7 uts have formulated the victim compensation scheme. the government has introduced a central victim compensation fund (cvcf) scheme, with an initial corpus of rs.200 crores, to enable support to victims of rape, acid attacks, human trafficking and women killed or injured in the cross-border firing. till now, there has been an absurd disparity in compensation amount paid by state governments varying from rs10,000 to rs10 lakh.

page_content: under the protection of children from sexual offences (pocso) act, victims of sexual abuse are entitled to compensation and relief measures. these include interim compensation for immediate needs and final compensation for long-term rehabilitation, determined by the special court. the delhi high court has mandated a minimum compensation of rs 10.5 lakh, with 25% paid as interim relief within two months of the charge sheet filing. senior citizens receive various reliefs and benefits in india, primarily through tax exemptions and deductions, and government schemes. these include higher exemption limits, standard deductions, tax rebates, and increased deductions for medical expenses. additionally, they are eligible for benefits like the ayushman bharat pradhan mantri jan arogya yojana (ab pm-jay) for health coverage and the indira gandhi national old age pension scheme (ignoaps). * * * the media's nature is defined by its objectivity , independence , and responsibility . - informative nature: media's primary duty is to inform the public accurately and fairly about events, developments, and issues of public concern. - educational role: it educates society by explaining complex issues like laws, rights, and reforms in simple language. - investigative function: media sometimes uncovers corruption, crime, and injustice, thereby supporting the rule of law. - persuasive power: through editorials and debates, media can influence public opinion and policy decisions. - entertainment role: it also provides cultural and recreational content, but overemphasis on entertainment can dilute its seriousness. thus, the media's nature is both informative and influential, with the potential for both positive and negative social impact. media profoundly influences society in multiple dimensions-social, political, cultural, and psychological. media plays a significant role in spreading knowledge about issues like gender equality, human rights, environmental protection, and public health. for example, campaigns like 'beti bachao, beti padhao' or 'swachh bharat' gained momentum through media coverage. media has the power to shape how people think about politics, governance, and justice. it can promote unity or create division, depending on the content and tone of coverage. by choosing which stories to highlight, media decides what the public perceives as important. this 'agenda-setting' function can direct national attention toward crime, corruption, or social reforms. excessive focus on violent crimes, sensational murder cases, or terrorism can sometimes create unnecessary fear among citizens, known as media-induced moral panic.

page_content: media promotes cultural integration but also risks eroding traditional values through the over-glorification of materialism, celebrity culture, and violence. media trials, or the tendency to pronounce judgment before the court's verdict, can prejudice ongoing cases and affect fair trial principles. at the same time, responsible journalism can ensure accountability by exposing misconduct or negligence. the criminal justice system consists of three main pillars-police, judiciary, and correctional institutions. media interacts with all three at various levels. media serves as both a partner and critic of the police. - it helps the police by disseminating information about missing persons, stolen vehicles, wanted criminals, and public safety warnings. - it creates pressure for timely action and transparency in investigation. - media coverage often leads to public cooperation in solving crimes. however, irresponsible reporting-such as leaking investigation details, revealing victim identities, or distorting facts-can hinder police work. media reports court proceedings and judgments, educating citizens about law and justice. yet, premature media commentary can influence witnesses or the public, risking contempt of court or miscarriage of justice. balanced coverage is essential. through documentaries and human-interest stories, media can highlight prison conditions, rehabilitation programs, and reforms needed in correctional administration. media exposes crimes, corruption, and abuse of power by government officials or private entities. investigative journalism has uncovered many scams and human rights violations, prompting legal and administrative reforms. conversely, sensational or misleading media coverage can sometimes inspire imitation crimes (copycat behavior), glorify criminals, or encourage vigilante justice. modern policing requires effective communication strategies. media-especially social media-can be a powerful tool for prevention, community policing, and transparency. police can use newspapers, television, and digital platforms to run awareness campaigns about: - cyber safety - road safety - drug abuse prevention - domestic violence reporting - child protection helplines example: hyderabad city police's hawk eye app was widely promoted through media to encourage public participation in safety monitoring. regular press briefings about major investigations, arrests, and preventive measures build public trust and prevent rumours. transparency through media enhances police credibility. official police accounts on x (twitter) , facebook, and instagram now act as interactive platforms for grievance redressal, missing persons tracing, and alert messaging. example: the telangana police uses social media to issue real-time traffic updates, missing child alerts, and emergency contacts. during riots, natural disasters, or emergencies, quick dissemination of verified information through media prevents panic and misinformation. police coordination with media ensures accurate public guidance. media can highlight positive policing efforts-such as blood donation camps, women's safety programs, and youth counseling-thus building a peoplefriendly image of the police. by showcasing professionalism and achievements through documentaries or news reports, police can inspire young citizens and motivate internal staff toward higher standards of integrity and service.

page_content: for effective cooperation, both police and media must follow ethical guidelines: - avoid revealing confidential investigation details. - protect victims' and juveniles' identities (as per pocso and jj acts). - refrain from sensationalism or bias in reporting. - maintain accuracy and verify facts before publication. - police should brief media officially, not through leaks. balanced, ethical collaboration ensures justice and public confidence. the media and press are indispensable to a transparent, democratic society. they not only inform and educate citizens but also hold institutions accountable. their role in crime reporting, awareness creation, and public cooperation strengthens the criminal justice system. however, the power of media must be exercised with responsibility. sensationalism, bias, and unverified reporting can damage reputations and obstruct justice. similarly, police officers must learn to manage media relations professionally-neither avoiding nor misusing it. when used ethically and strategically, media becomes a strong ally of the police in crime prevention, community trust-building, and justice delivery. a well-informed public and a transparent police force together form the foundation of a safe and democratic society. * * * crime prevention is the set of deliberate actions taken to reduce the incidence, fear and impact of crime before offences occur. clear objectives guide operational choices and evaluation. core objectives are: - reduce incidence of crime - lower the number of offences through interventions (deterrence, removal of opportunities, targeted policing). - protect vulnerable groups and places - ensure women, children, elderly and high-risk localities are safer. - reduce harm and recidivism - focus on lessening the social and personal harm caused by crime and preventing re-offending. - increase community safety and confidence - reduce fear of crime and strengthen police-public trust. - improve detection & response capacity -through faster reporting, intelligence and evidence collection so offences are prevented from escalating. - address root causes - link policing to social, educational and economic interventions that remove drivers of offending. these objectives shape tactical choices: some aim to deter (visible policing), some to incapacitate (arrests/hotspot crackdowns), some to design out crime (environmental design), and others to engage communities for co-production of safety. telangana police use a blended approach - conventional policing plus modern, technology-enabled and community-facing strategies. key pillars are: community policing (neighbourhood engagement), specialised units for vulnerable groups, smart/tech policing (geo-mapping, apps, cctv/anpr, predictive analytics), and institutional capacity building. the state has institutionalised women-focused structures (women safety wing, she teams, bharosa centres) and rolled out apps and geo-tools to improve reporting and situational awareness.

page_content: - a) community policing and neighbourhood watches: telangana places emphasis on building community ties - creating resident/neighbourhood watch groups, outreach camps, school and college safety programs, and police public meetings. these schemes aim to create local 'eyes and ears', encourage timely reporting, and reduce social tolerance for minor public order offences. the state actively promotes formation of such citizen groups via social media and district campaigns. - b) women-centred units: she teams & bharosa: she teams are specialised mobile squads that patrol public spaces, act on complaints of eveteasing/stalking and intervene rapidly in women safety incidents; they work with bharosa/bharosa centres (support, counselling, forensic help). the women safety wing coordinates anti-human-trafficking units, cyber labs and missing-person monitoring. these focused units both provide support services and act preventively by visible deterrence and outreach. telangana has invested in apps and platforms (e.g., hawk eye, other citizen reporting apps), integrated cctv networks, geo-spatial crime mapping and trials of predictive analytics to identify hotspots and allocate resources. these tools allow faster citizen reporting, automated alerts, and data visualization for commanders to deploy beat staff or mobile teams to anticipated hotspots. however, the use of facial recognition and similar surveillance tools has drawn legal and privacy scrutiny, so technology deployment is accompanied by public debate and calls for oversight. - d) problem-oriented and hotspot policing: analytical teams examine recurring problems (e.g., persistent night-time robbery at a bus stop) and design tailored interventions - environmental changes, focused patrols, working with local municipal services (lighting, vendor management), and targeted enforcement. hotspot policing increases patrol density in higher-risk places and times to reduce opportunity and detect offenders. - e) rapid response & reporting systems: emergency response (dial-100 and newer mobile channels), integrated call centers, and social-media/whatsapp complaint intake enable faster intervention. rapid, visible responses are a key deterrent and increase public trust in reporting. - f) outreach, education and awareness campaigns: the police conduct regular awareness drives (school/college talks, traffic safety, cyber-safety workshops, anti-drug campaigns) and use local media and social platforms to communicate dos and don'ts, locations of help centres, and how to report. these reduce victimization risk and mobilize community support. - g) inter-agency coordination & victim support: telangana's model links police with prosecution, social services, ngos and district administrations - especially for aht (anti-human-trafficking), missing persons, and women/child protection. bharosa centres are an example of co-located multi-disciplinary support. - beat and patrol policing: regular foot and mobile patrols increase police presence, provide informal intelligence, and reassure the public. focused night patrols and visible movement in marketplaces, transport hubs and college areas are widely used. - hotspot & problem-oriented policing: data identifies micro-areas and times with concentrated incidents; resources are allocated to disrupt patterns (patrols, target hardening, situational fixes). - community co-production & neighbourhood watches: recruit and train local volunteers to report suspicious activity, manage watch groups, and assist in community safety audits. - situational crime prevention (environmental design): work with municipal bodies to improve street lighting, clear vegetation, manage vendor layouts, install signage and cctv at risk points to reduce opportunities. - intelligence-led policing & data analytics: use crime databases, mobileapp reports, cctv feeds, and geospatial analysis to prioritize targets, anticipate crime trends and deploy teams proactively. - technology tools - apps, cctv, anpr, facial recognition, predictive models: citizen apps (hawk eye, others), integrated city cctv, automated number plate recognition for vehicle tracking, and analytics are used to speed detection and deter repeat offenders. predictive models are used experimentally to prioritize patrols but must be balanced against bias and privacy concerns. - specialised response units: she teams, cyber labs, anti-humantrafficking units and quick action teams focus on specific crime types requiring specialist skills and sensitivity. legal enforcement and visible sanctions: enforcement of public order laws, strict action on repeat offenders (e.g., using relevant penal statutes), and publicized success stories help deter. victim support and rehabilitation linkages: bharosa centres, counselling and legal aid reduce secondary victimization and improve willingness to cooperate which strengthens prevention by ensuring cases are reported and prosecuted. - integrated model: combination of community policing + targeted units + tech tools improves coverage and responsiveness.

page_content: - women-safety focus: institutional structures (she teams, bharosa) provide both deterrence and support. - privacy & civil-liberties concerns: facial recognition and pervasive surveillance have been legally contested; transparency, oversight and clear rules are essential. - algorithmic bias & over-reliance on tech: predictive tools can entrench biases if fed biased historical data - human oversight and periodic audits are needed. - resource and training gaps: sustained community policing requires training, staffing and local language/ cultural skills. - coordination with social services: prevention that addresses root causes needs stronger partnership with education, mental health and employment programs. - transparent tech governance: publish policy on use of cctv, facial recognition, data retention and grievance redressal. - community capacity building: invest in sustained neighbourhood watch training and formal liaison officers for citizen groups. - evidence-led evaluation : assess what reduces crime (not just arrests) using independent studies and adjust tactics. - safeguards for vulnerable groups: ensure bharosa and she teams have social-work backup and trauma-informed training. - audit predictive systems : independent audits for accuracy, bias and fairness; keep humans in decision loops. - environmental fixes : partner with municipal agencies for lighting, signage and transport safety interventions. telangana police combine traditional patrol work, community engagement and a visible focus on women's safety with a growing suite of digital tools to prevent crime. the model's promise lies in integrating these pillars - technology for speed and coverage, community policing for legitimacy and human intelligence, and specialised units for vulnerable groups. to keep that promise, the state must pair innovation with robust safeguards: transparency, accountability, audit of algorithms, and investments in community outreach and victim support. when these elements align, crime prevention becomes not only a policing task but a shared civic activity that reduces harm and strengthens public trust. community policing is a law enforcement strategy that has been around since the early 80s in the united states. most people in the public safety sector have likely heard this term before, but what does community policing really mean? community policing is generally defined as a law enforcement philosophy that allows officers to continuously operate in the same area in order to create a stronger bond with the citizens living and working in that area. this allows public safety officers to engage with local residents and prevent crime from happening instead of responding to incidents after they occur. - intended to prevent crime before it happens rather than responding to crime after it occurs - focuses on creating a safe social environment - engages residents to determine which criminal activities they are most affected by, creating an accurate law enforcement priority list shaped by the people who live in the area - encourages residents to participate with law enforcement in order to keep their own community safe. in its simplest form, community policing creates a partnership between law enforcement and residents. the more involved law enforcement is with the residents they are sworn to protect, the more residents can help law enforcement achieve their goals. - residents having a more favorable view of their local police department - improved trust between law enforcement and residents - more accurate information from residents regarding criminal activity in their community - better understanding of the needs of citizens and their expectations of the police many public safety agencies are using technology to better engage their residents and boost their community policing initiatives. systems like ever bridge nixle use tools such as mass notification, 2-way communication, anonymous tipping, and social media integration to engage residents on the platforms and devices they use most.

page_content: police-public cooperation is crucial for effective crime prevention, investigation, and the overall maintenance of law and order. it involves a collaborative effort where the public actively participates with law enforcement agencies by sharing information, reporting crimes, and offering assistance. this partnership fosters trust and legitimacy in the police, empowering them to better serve the community and enhance public safety. - effective crime prevention: public cooperation helps identify potential crime hotspots and enables proactive policing strategies. - enhanced investigation: sharing information about crimes, such as witnessing suspicious activity or providing witness statements, aids in successful investigations. - strengthened trust and legitimacy: positive interactions and collaboration build trust and legitimacy in the police, making citizens more likely to cooperate in the future. - community safety: when the public and police work together, the community benefits from a safer and more secure environment. - improved police performance: community input and feedback can inform policy development and improve police practices. - increased accountability: the public can hold the police accountable through various channels, including social media and citizen forums. - citizen intelligence networks: empowering ordinary citizens to report suspicious activity and provide information to the police. - community policing programs: establishing partnerships between the police and community organizations to address local concerns and build trust. - police-community forums: creating platforms for open dialogue and collaboration between the police and residents. - reporting crimes: encouraging individuals to report crimes, especially through non-emergency channels. - supporting police efforts: citizens can actively participate in local safety initiatives, such as neighborhood watch programs. - perception of fairness and legality: citizens are more likely to cooperate with the police if they perceive them as fair, legitimate, and transparent. - trust and confidence: a strong police-public relationship built on trust and mutual respect is essential for effective cooperation. - accountability: when the police are held accountable for their actions, it fosters public trust and encourages collaboration. - public education: educating the public about their rights and responsibilities in relation to the police can promote cooperation. the "smart police vision" in india, coined by the prime minister in 2014, aims to modernize and enhance policing by embracing technology, improving efficiency, and fostering accountability. it's about transforming the police force to be "smart" - strict and sensitive, modern and mobile, alert and accountable, reliable and responsive, and trained and techno-savvy. - strict and sensitive: enforcing laws effectively while maintaining sensitivity towards citizens' needs. - modern and mobile: utilizing technology and adapting to changing crime trends. - alert and accountable: proactive crime prevention and ensuring transparency in operations. - reliable and responsive: providing timely and effective assistance to the public. - trained and techno-savvy: investing in training and equipping officers with the latest technology. - technology adoption: implementing advanced technologies like ai, big data analytics, and drone surveillance for predictive policing and crime prevention. - modernization of infrastructure: upgrading police stations, equipping them with cctv cameras, and establishing forensic labs. - capacity building: providing intensive training for police officials, including specialized training in areas like cybercrime. - focus on citizen engagement: promoting community policing and fostering trust between the police and the public. - accountability and transparency: enhancing police accountability through internal audits and external oversight. - focus on vulnerable sections: protecting the rights and interests of marginalized communities and ensuring access to justice.

page_content: - gandhinagar smart police city project: a pilot project to integrate technology and best practices into policing. - use of ai and big data analytics: predictive policing to identify crime hotspots and allocate resources effectively. - modernization of cybercrime cells: tackling online crimes and cyber fraud. - establishment of forensic labs: providing scientific evidence for investigations. - improved crime prevention and response: more effective policing strategies and timely interventions. - enhanced public trust and confidence: accountable and responsive policing leads to greater trust. - modernized and efficient police force: technology and training enhance operational efficiency. - protection of vulnerable sections: smart policing ensures that the needs of all citizens are addressed. the international perspective of policing involves understanding how different countries approach law enforcement, including their roles, responsibilities, and organizational structures. this also includes understanding the principles of international cooperation and the challenges of policing in a globalized world. - democratic policing: many countries, especially those with democratic systems, emphasize community policing, where police work closely with the public and prioritize public trust. - authoritarian policing: in some countries, policing may be more militarized and centralized, with a focus on maintaining order and suppressing dissent. - specialized policing: many countries have specialized units, such as those focusing on mental health crises, cybercrime, or counter-terrorism. - police integrity and accountability: international bodies like the united nations emphasize the importance of strong oversight mechanisms and a culture of transparency within police forces. - international cooperation: organizations like interpol facilitate cooperation between police forces across borders, particularly in addressing transnational crime. - police use of force: the basic principles on the use of force and firearms by law enforcement officials guide the use of force by police, emphasizing proportionality and the minimization of harm. - police education and training: international training programs and partnerships help to build capacity and share best practices among police forces. - transnational crime: globalization has facilitated the movement of criminals and the spread of organized crime, requiring international cooperation to address these challenges. - cybercrime: the rise of cybercrime and online threats requires specialized training and cooperation among police forces to combat this new form of crime. - terrorism: international terrorist organizations operate across borders, requiring close collaboration among law enforcement agencies to prevent attacks and disrupt their operations. - public trust and legitimacy: in many countries, police face challenges in building and maintaining public trust, particularly in the wake of controversial police actions. - united nations: the un plays a key role in supporting peace and security by providing police training and assistance to countries in conflict or postconflict situations. - interpol: these international criminal police organization facilitates cooperation between police forces, particularly in the exchange of information and the pursuit of criminals across borders. - world organization of police chiefs: this organization provides a platform for police chiefs from different countries to share information, best practices, and address common challenges. the judiciary's attitude towards society encompasses its role in upholding the law, protecting fundamental rights, and ensuring social order. it involves a balance of impartiality, independence, and a commitment to justice and fairness, reflecting a deep-seated faith in the judicial system and its ability to address societal issues. the judiciary also plays a crucial role in shaping societal norms and beliefs through its rulings and interpretations of the law, as seen in cases involving issues like homosexuality and caste discrimination.

page_content: - upholding the rule of law: the judiciary enforces laws, ensuring that everyone is subject to and accountable under the law. - protecting fundamental rights: the judiciary safeguards citizens' rights, acting as a guardian of the constitution and protecting individuals from potential abuses of power by the state or other entities. - ensuring social order: the judiciary plays a vital role in maintaining social order and resolving disputes peacefully, preventing chaos and instability. - impartiality and independence: judges are expected to be impartial and unbiased, acting as neutral arbiters in legal proceedings and free from undue influence. - judicial activism: in some cases, the judiciary has taken a proactive role in addressing societal issues, expanding the scope of legal protection and promoting social change. - balancing individual rights and societal interests: the judiciary navigates the complex interplay between individual freedoms and the collective good, seeking to balance the needs of both. - shaping societal norms and beliefs: judicial rulings and interpretations can influence public opinion and cultural norms, impacting issues such as lgbtq+ rights, caste discrimination, and social justice. - ethical conduct: judges are expected to maintain high ethical standards, both in their official and personal conduct, fostering public trust and confidence in the judiciary. - respect for other branches of government: the judiciary operates within the framework of the constitution, respecting the roles and functions of the legislative and executive branches. - judicial independence: the judiciary's independence is essential for ensuring fair trials and preventing political interference in judicial decisions. * * * - reduced recidivism: effective rehabilitation programs can significantly reduce the likelihood of offenders re-offending, contributing to lower crime rates. - improved quality of life: rehabilitation can help offenders improve their mental health, build social connections, and develop skills that enable them to lead productive lives. - cost savings: while rehabilitation programs require resources, they can ultimately save society money by reducing crime rates and the need for incarceration. - community benefits: by reintegrating offenders into society, rehabilitation can help restore social bonds and reduce the negative impact of crime on communities. - effective caseload management ensures timely justice and better public service. - overburdened police forces suffer from delayed investigations and errors. - high caseloads reduce police efficiency and increase public dissatisfaction. - mismanaged cases lead to poor conviction rates and loss of trust. - managing caseloads is critical for upholding constitutional rights and the rule of law. - enables police to focus on priority cases, such as serious crimes or vulnerable victims. - improves resource utilization by preventing backlog accumulation. - essential for data-driven policing and smart allocation of personnel. - aids in identifying patterns in criminal behavior or repeat offenders. - contributes to organizational accountability and transparency. - introduce triaging of cases-prioritize based on severity and solvability. - promote alternative dispute resolution (adr) for minor offenses. - increase reliance on technology and automation in filing and tracking. - assign specialized units for specific crime types (e.g., cybercrime, narcotics). - encourage public-private partnerships for infrastructure and support services.

page_content: - train officers in time management, digital literacy, and investigative efficiency. - expand the use of community policing and shared responsibility models. - reduce non-core burdens (e.g., vip duties, civil disputes) through delegation. - implement performance metrics and accountability systems. - strengthen coordination between police, judiciary, and prosecution for faster case disposal. - case management software enables real-time tracking and status updates. - use of e-beat books and mobile apps to reduce paperwork. - integration with crime and criminal tracking network systems (cctns) for faster data access. - digital evidence storage and forensic tools streamline investigation. - automated alerts for pending tasks, court dates, and follow-ups. - use of gis and crime mapping for focused patrolling and deployment. - predictive policing algorithms can reduce workload by forecasting crime trends. - centralized databases improve information sharing across districts and agencies. - mobile devices help officers report and record on-the-go. - online fir systems reduce unnecessary footfall at stations and speed up registration. - develop a monitoring mechanism for case allocation and closure timelines. - establish a reporting hierarchy for complex or sensitive investigations. - require weekly/monthly reviews of open cases and pending investigations. - create dedicated administrative staff for non-policing tasks. - introduce a system for inter-departmental feedback and escalation. - enable decentralized decision-making at police station level. - implement case diaries and audit trails to ensure accountability. - reduce frequent transfers of investigating officers, which disrupt continuity. - maintain a database of pending charge sheets and unexplained delays. - make caseload statistics public to ensure transparency and responsiveness. [ * * * human rights are the basic, inherent, inalienable, and universal rights that belong to every individual simply because they are human. they ensure dignity, equality, and freedom for all persons irrespective of nationality, caste, gender, religion, race, or status. in the field of policing, respect for human rights is not an optional value but a constitutional and professional obligation. law enforcement agencies are both protectors and enforcers of these rights. every act of arrest, interrogation, search, or detention must align with the principles of human dignity and rule of law. - meaning: human rights are the minimum rights which every individual must have against the state or other public authorities by virtue of being human. - un definition (1948): 'human rights are those rights which are inherent in our nature and without which we cannot live as human beings.' - indian national human rights commission (nhrc) definition : 'human rights are the rights relating to life, liberty, equality, and dignity of the individual guaranteed by the constitution or embodied in international covenants and enforceable by courts in india.' - inherent: they belong to individuals from birth; no one grants them. - universal: apply equally to all human beings everywhere. - inalienable: cannot be taken away, except under lawful and justified circumstances. - interdependent: rights like liberty, equality, and dignity are interconnected. - indivisible: civil, political, social, and economic rights are equally important. - dynamic: evolve with social, economic, and political developments. human rights stem from several schools of thought:

page_content: - natural law theory: rights are derived from human nature and reason (john locke, rousseau). - liberalism: emphasizes individual liberty and limited government. - social justice theory: stresses equality, fair distribution, and welfare (karl marx). - utilitarianism: rights promote the greatest happiness for the greatest number (bentham, mill). - modern constitutionalism: integrates all above theories into legal and institutional guarantees. - ancient civilizations (greek, roman, indian, chinese) recognized moral duties and justice but not individual rights as we know them today. - ashoka's edicts (3rd century bce) in india emphasized compassion, tolerance, and welfare - early signs of humanist governance. - in europe, concepts of natural rights and justice were discussed by philosophers like aristotle and cicero. - first formal assertion of rights against arbitrary authority. - limited the power of the king and established the principle of due process of law. bill of rights (1689, england): affirmed parliamentary supremacy and fundamental liberties like freedom of speech, religion, and fair trial. american declaration of independence (1776): declared that all men are created equal and endowed with unalienable rights - 'life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness.' - recognized liberty, property, security, and resistance to oppression as natural rights. - basis for modern human rights discourse. - abolition of slavery, industrial reforms, and women's suffrage movements expanded the scope of rights. - emergence of labour rights , education rights, and social welfare measures . - the atrocities of world war ii, including genocide, led to global recognition of the need for universal protection of human dignity. - the united nations (un) was established in 1945 with the goal of promoting peace, justice, and human rights. - the first international treaty to recognize human rights as a universal concern. - articles 1, 55, and 56 emphasize respect for human rights and fundamental freedoms. - adopted on 10 december 1948 - celebrated as human rights day . - not legally binding but morally authoritative and foundational for later treaties. - divided into civil, political, economic, social, and cultural rights. - art. 1: all human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. - art. 3: right to life, liberty, and security. - art. 5: no torture or cruel treatment. - art. 9: no arbitrary arrest, detention, or exile. - art. 19: freedom of expression. - art. 26: right to education. - iccpr (international covenant on civil and political rights): protects life, liberty, fair trial, expression, religion, and political participation. - icescr (international covenant on economic, social and cultural rights): - ensures right to work, education, health, and adequate standard of living. together with the udhr, these form the international bill of human rights . - convention on the elimination of racial discrimination (1965) - convention on the elimination of discrimination against women (cedaw, 1979)

page_content: - convention against torture (cat, 1984) - convention on the rights of the child (crc, 1989) - convention on rights of persons with disabilities (2006) - concepts of 'dharma' , 'ahimsa' , and 'sarvodaya' in vedic and buddhist thought emphasized respect for all beings. - kautilya's arthashastra and manusmriti prescribed duties of rulers to protect subjects and ensure justice. - bhagavad gita upheld equality of souls and moral conduct. - british rule witnessed suppression but also introduction of rule of law and constitutionalism. - indian reformers like raja ram mohan roy , swami vivekananda , and mahatma gandhi emphasized social equality, abolition of untouchability, women's rights, and freedom. - the indian national movement under gandhi based itself on truth, nonviolence, and human dignity. after independence (1947), india adopted its constitution in 1950 , which enshrines a comprehensive set of rights. preamble: promises justice, liberty, equality, and fraternity - the foundation of human rights. - articles 12-35 guarantee civil and political rights. - right to equality (art. 14-18) - right to freedom (art. 19-22) - right against exploitation (art. 23-24) - right to freedom of religion (art. 25-28) - cultural and educational rights (art. 29-30) - right to constitutional remedies (art. 32) -called the heart and soul of the constitution by dr. b.r. ambedkar. - socio-economic rights - employment, health, education, equal pay, and living wage. - though non-justiciable, they guide state policy and legislation. - emphasize individual responsibility towards the community and national integrity. the supreme court and high courts have played a key role in expanding human rights through liberal interpretation of the constitution. - maneka gandhi vs. union of india (1978): expanded article 21 - right to life includes dignity and fair procedure. - hussainara khatoon vs. state of bihar (1979): right to speedy trial recognized. - d.k. basu vs. state of west bengal (1997): laid down arrest and detention guidelines for police. - vishaka vs. state of rajasthan (1997): recognized sexual harassment at workplace as a violation of gender equality. - right to privacy (puttaswamy case, 2017): declared privacy as a fundamental right. national human rights commission (nhrc) - established under the protection of human rights act, 1993. - composition: chairperson (former chief justice of india), members from judiciary, and experts. - powers: - o inquire into human rights violations by public servants. - o recommend actions and compensation. - o review constitutional safeguards. - o promote research, education, and awareness. function at the state level to address local human rights issues.

page_content: - national commission for women (ncw) - national commission for minorities (ncm) - national commission for scheduled castes/tribes (ncsc/st) - national commission for protection of child rights (ncpcr) police are the frontline defenders of human rights. their actions directly affect the liberty and dignity of citizens. every arrest, search, interrogation, or encounter has a human rights dimension. police obligations: - respect constitutional rights during enforcement. - avoid torture, illegal detention, and custodial violence. - ensure humane treatment of accused, witnesses, and victims. - maintain gender sensitivity, especially towards women and children. - follow d.k. basu guidelines during arrest: - inform family of the person arrested. - maintain arrest memo. - medical examination every 48 hours. - right to legal counsel. - excessive use of force. - encounter killings. - custodial deaths and torture. - discrimination or bias in law enforcement. - continuous human rights training in police academies. - strict accountability and internal vigilance. - use of technology (cctv in lockups, body cameras). - community policing and transparency. - movements for dalit rights, tribal rights, women's empowerment, rti, environment protection , and lgbtq+ equality reflect the expanding horizon of human rights. - entry in diary: an entry must be made in the diary about who was informed of the arrest. this ensures transparency and accountability in the arrest process. - judicial oversight: the magistrate, before whom the arrested person is produced, must verify that these requirements have been met. this judicial oversight aims to prevent arbitrary detention and protect individual liberty. - reasonable justification for arrest: arrests should be made based on reasonable satisfaction regarding the genuineness of the complaint and the person's involvement in the offence. arbitrary arrests based on mere suspicion or allegations must be avoided. prakash singh reforms (2006): police accountability, separation of investigation and law & order, transparent postings, and insulating police from political pressure. important nhrc guidelines include: - procedure for encounter deaths - mandatory magisterial inquiry for custodial deaths - reporting of custodial deaths within 24 hours - videography of post-mortems - instructions on use of force a human-rights-centric investigation includes: - no torture or third-degree - evidence-based, forensic-driven enquiry - maintaining case diary honestly - recording statements lawfully (section 161 & 164 crpc) - avoiding illegal detention fair investigation is a constitutional requirement (article 21). community policing builds trust and reduces conflict. - interaction with local communities - involving ngos in awareness programs - grievance redressal mechanisms - transparency in working when citizens trust the police, human rights violations automatically decline.

page_content: police must follow: - jj act procedures for children - posh act guidelines in station - pocso provisions for child victims - use of women officers for search, arrest, counselling sensitivity and empathy are critical skills for a dsp. regular training is essential in: - human rights law - counseling and conflict resolution - forensic techniques - ethical behaviour - documentation and procedural safeguards - training reduces dependence on outdated methods. - cctv in police stations - complaint boxes and helplines - social media transparency - regular inspection of lockups - recording of searches and seizures - body-worn cameras during operations - departments that are transparent earn greater public trust. use of force must be: - necessary - proportionate - minimum required - as per sop police must adopt empathetic approaches for: - sc/st communities - women and children - migrants - senior citizens - lgbtq+ individuals - mentally ill persons respect for dignity must guide all interactions. 1. follow crpc sections 41 & 41a strictly before arrest. 2. ensure cctv with audio-video recording in all police stations. 3. implement zero-tolerance policy for custodial torture. 4. ensure mandatory medical examination of accused. 5. conduct fair, impartial, and scientific investigations . 6. comply with nhrc encounter guidelines, magisterial inquiries. 7. ensure legal aid, counsel access, and communication with family. 8. maintain station diary, arrest memo, and visitor register accurately. 9. follow gender-sensitive protocols for searches, interrogations. 10. conduct periodic human rights training for subordinates. 11. encourage community policing, social media accountability. 12. promote ethical and corruption-free policing. 13. conduct surprise inspections of stations and lockups. 14. use non-coercive interrogation techniques. 15. ensure respect for privacy, confidentiality of victims. these guidelines reflect modern policing standards aligned with democratic values. human rights-centric policing is not a burden but a professional responsibility that enhances credibility, strengthens investigations, and ensures justice. for a new dsp, balancing enforcement with empathy, power with restraint, and authority with accountability is crucial. a police officer who respects human rights gains public trust, reduces conflict, and encourages cooperation-making policing easier and more effective. a humane police force is the foundation of a strong justice system. modern india expects police officers to be guardians of rights, protectors of dignity, and ambassadors of justice. the true measure of policing is not how much power the police exercise, but how responsibly that power is used to safeguard human rights.

page_content: the universal declaration of human rights (udhr) is an international document adopted by the united nations general assembly (unga). it establishes the rights and freedoms of all members of the human race. it was accepted by the unga as per resolution 217 during the session on december 10, 1948. among the united nations members at the time, 48 voted in favour, none against, 8 abstained and 2 did not vote. - the udhr consists of 30 articles detailing an individual's 'basic rights and fundamental freedoms'. it is universally applicable for all human beings of varying race, religions and nationality. - it directly inspired the development of international human rights law, and was the first step in the formulation of the international bill of human rights, which was completed in 1966 and came into force in 1976. - even though the universal human rights declaration is not legally binding, its contents has been elaborated and incorporated into subsequent international treaties, regional human rights and instruments and in the legal codes of various countries - at least one of the 9 binding treaties of the udhr has been ratified by all 193 member states of the united nations, with the majority ratifying four or more. the united nations was founded by 51 countries in october 1945, two months after world war ii ended. two world wars, the nuclear bombings of hiroshima and nagasaki and a global refugee crisis had led to fears of a destructive world war ii. the un was founded to avoid such a disaster, as well as to address human rights. out of all the people who wanted such notions to become a reality, it was eleanor roosevelt - the wife of the late united states president franklin delano roosevelt - who would play a crucial role in the formulation of the universal human rights declaration president harry truman appointed eleanor roosevelt to the us delegation to the united nations in 1945. she was well known throughout the world as a champion of poverty allegations and universal civil rights. it was in april 1946, after becoming chair of the un commission on human rights, that she took on the task of drafting a human rights declaration for the world. eleanor's ideals about human rights and desire for global peace were influenced by her experiences of both the world wars where she had worked with shell-shocked soldiers undergoing psychological treatments during world war-i while she had visited the devastated cities of europe during the second world war. 1. both the united states and the soviet union had their own definition of human or to put it simply could not agree on what human rights were. 2. many conservative us politicians were not fond of supporting the economic and social rights of the udhr because in their eyes such rights were 'communist' in their nature and scope. however, elanor with her charm and diplomacy managed to gather enough support for the udhr to be passed in a resolution. hansa mehta, a un delegate from the newly independent country of india and the only other woman on the commission on human rights was crucial in shaping the declaration. it was she who changed the original declaration's first article from 'all men are born free and equal' to 'all human beings are born free and equal'. even though the declaration isn't binding or enforceable. it would serve as a model for legislation in many countries. after the draft was presented to the united nations general assembly, it was adopted on december 10, 1946. december 10, the anniversary of the adoption of the universal declaration, is celebrated annually as world human rights day or international human rights day. the national human rights commission or nhrc is a standalone entity of the government of india with the mission of promoting and protecting human rights. it is a statutory body mentioned in the constitution of india that was established in 1993 under the 'protection of human rights act.' this act was further amended in 2006. the national human rights commission (nhrc) established in 1993, is an independent statutory body as per the provisions of the protection of human rights act of 1993 which was amended in 2006. - human rights are an indispensable part of society and human rights in india are watched by nhrc. - nhrc acts as a watchdog of human rights in the country.

page_content: - nhrc looks over the rights that are related to life, dignity, liberty and equality of the individual that is defined in section 2(1) of the phr act. - they are guaranteed by the constitution of india, embodied in the international covenants and are enforceable by the courts of india as well. - nhrc was established in compliance with the paris principles of human rights, 1991 which were adopted for the promotion and protection of human rights and were endorsed by the united nations at its general assembly of 1993 - in 1948, the un adopted the udhr (universal declaration of human rights). - in 1991, the paris principles were established by the national human rights institutions (nhris). - in 1993, the un adopted these paris principles at its general assembly. - in 1993, india enacted the protection of human rights act. - this led to the formation of the national human rights commission (nhrc). - the protection of human rights act also allowed state governments to establish the state human rights commission. - the national human rights commission (nhrc) is composed of a chairperson and eight other members. - those eight members are: - four full-time members. - four deemed members. chairman of nhrc, composition of nhrc = retired chief justice of india. member 1, composition of nhrc = one who is/has been a judge of supreme court of india or one who is/has been a chief justice of a high court. three members, composition of nhrc = candidates with the knowledge or practical experience in the matters of human rights (at least one to be a woman member). deemed members (ex-officio members), composition of nhrc = deemed members are chairpersons of the below national commissions: national commission for minorities national commission for scheduled castes - a selection committee will recommend the candidates to the president. - the selection committee includes: - prime minister (chairman) - speaker of lok sabha - union home minister - deputy chairman of rajya sabha - leaders of the opposition in both houses of the parliament the functions of the national human rights commission (nhrc) as stated in section 12 of the protection of human rights act,1993 includes enquiry into complaints of violation of human rights or negligence in the prevention of such violation by a public servant. the commission also studies treaties and international instruments on human rights and makes recommendations for their effective implementation to the government. - nhrc can investigate any complaints related to violations of human rights in india either suo-moto or after receiving a petition. - nhrc can interfere in any judicial process that involves any allegation of violation of human rights. - it can visit any prison/institute under the control of the state governments to observe the living conditions of inmates. it can further make recommendations based on its observations to the authorities. - nhrc can review the provisions of the constitution that safeguard human rights and can suggest necessary restorative measures. - research in the field of human rights is also promoted by the nhrc. - human rights awareness and literacy through different media are promoted by nhrc in various sectors of society. - nhrc has the power to recommend suitable steps that can prevent violation of human rights in india to both central as well as state governments. - the president of india gets an annual report from nhrc which is laid before both the houses of the parliament.

page_content: it is important to know the limitations of nhrc for the upsc exam. they are mentioned below: - the recommendations made by the nhrc are not binding. - violation of human rights by private parties cannot be considered under nhrc jurisdiction. - nhrc doesn't have the power to penalize the authorities that don't implement its recommended orders. - 3 of the nhrc members are judges which give the functioning of the commission a judicial touch. - the other members that are recommended by the selection committee may not necessarily be human rights experts. - the nhrc does not consider the following cases: - cases that are older than one year. - cases that are anonymous, pseudonymous or vague. - frivolous cases. - cases pertaining to service matters. - the nhrc has limited jurisdiction over cases related to armed forces. - the nhrc faces other issues like excess cases/complaints, insufficient funds, bureaucratic functioning style, etc. india faces human rights violations on a large scale due to various reasons. the national human rights commission (nhrc) takes up most of the issues around the country. some of them are mentioned below: - arbitrary arrest and detention - custodial torture - child labour - violence and discrimination against women and children - extrajudicial killings - excessive powers - sexual violence and abuse - lgbtq community rights - sc/st, disabled people and other religious minority issues - labour rights and right to work - conflict induced internal displacement - manual scavenging - 22,433 cases were registered, showing an increase of 9.2% over 2022. -
